And they asked me what is the secret to... Well the first question was, a script is important, right? I said damn straight they are. And they go what's the secret? I said the key is what's wrong? Why do most people either not use scripts in this negative connotation? I'll tell you what it is. The reason most people either don't use scripts or they have some negative anchor towards scripts is because they used scripts in the past that sucked, that weren't actually usable. They explained unless your script is perfect, meaning it's of the straight line variety where every word flows like butter, there's no tongue twisters. They make perfect sense. If there's something that's wrong, they're written too technically. If they're not written in the spoken word, they're written in the written word. Meaning that the way you write something in a book is very different than the way you say it. So if you have not written your script in a way that is written for the spoken word, which means lots of contractions and slang and using complicated words that might be just above their knowledge base, your prospects average knowledge base, but made so simple by the way you present them. You explain them that you sound great. You sound like a real expert. You see a real expert when they speak, they say things that while they sound complex, they make them really easy to understand. That's one of the secrets, one of the true secrets to writing a great script that when you speak it, it's like they're like, wow man, that was f***ing... Oh boy, like a couple of words in there, oh boy, but yet they understood every f***ing word. You understand? There's a big difference in writing a technically proficient pattern that sounds great, but no one knows what the f*** you were saying, right? That doesn't serve you. You're not going to close. It creates overwhelm, suspicion. They might be impressed that you're an expert, but not so that's relatable. A real expert knows how to take the complex and make it simple to understand. It still might sound complex a bit and stills like wow, but oh boy, that's really great. That's who people buy from. So please work on your scripts today. I want you to make... Why they should buy it. And I want you to make sure you want you to order them first, second, third pattern, and make sure that each pattern builds on the one before it. Remember you're framing a house. Frame builds up first on the drywall, right? Then you put the paint on. You don't put the paint on first or you make sure you got to run the electric behind the wall before you put the walls up, right? The same way you run in your frame, an anti-logical case. Big picture to small picture, right? Don't go into the nitty gritty first. Frame, don't front load. And each pattern builds on the one before it. That's the key. Also, remember in the beginning, your opening patterns need to have a very strong logical underpinning because you always create our airtight logical case first, right? Why? Because logic, the logical mind serves as the human bull detector. And if we don't do that, what happens when you try to move someone emotionally, their mind goes, the logic, you get their right brain is stopped from moving because the left brain needs the left brain, the intellectual brain says, wait a second, he hasn't made the airtight logical case. Something's bull. I'm trying to be hyped. So what we do is we create the airtight logical case first, right? And by, like, ah, okay, wow, yeah, that makes, I'm not buying, but boy does that make sense. Oh man, what a good, once you get someone thinking like that, like, yeah, makes perfect sense. I can connect all the dots, right? Then that frees them up to be moved emotionally. That's why the logic first, emotion second. And remember, each one picks up whether one prior left off. So when we write a script, we don't just write our initial presentation. We include our looping patterns. We include all our questions during qualifying.
